dependencies.reason: You need a trained model to evaluate — supervised learning teaches you how to train one.
Ensemble methods combine supervised models, so you need to understand individual models first.
Understanding supervised learning first gives you the mental model to contrast with unsupervised approaches.
Good features are the input to any ML model — engineering them well directly impacts model performance.
CNNs are specialized neural networks — you need to understand the base architecture first.
RNNs extend the basic neural network with recurrence — foundational concepts apply directly.
Transformers replace recurrence with attention, but share the same neural network building blocks.
LLMs are transformer-based models scaled to billions of parameters — transformers are their foundation.
CNNs are the primary architecture for image classification tasks.
Object detection builds on CNN feature extraction for localization.
Vision Transformers directly apply the transformer architecture to image patches.
GANs consist of two neural networks — you need to understand both first.
Diffusion models use neural networks for iterative denoising — foundational NN knowledge is required.
Text preprocessing produces the clean tokens that word embedding models are trained on.
Vector embeddings for RAG generalize the concept of word embeddings to full documents and sentences.
Prompt engineering is the art of communicating with LLMs — you need to understand what LLMs are first.
Fine-tuning adapts pre-trained LLMs — understanding the base model is a prerequisite.
RAG augments LLM generation with retrieval — you need to understand LLMs to know what you're augmenting.
Chunks are what get embedded — understanding embeddings helps you decide optimal chunk size and boundaries.
Retrieval operates on chunks — you need to understand chunking to reason about retrieval quality.
Vector databases are the storage backend for retrieval — you need to know what you're retrieving first.
Re-ranking improves the results from a vector database retrieval — you need retrieval working first.
Evaluating RAG requires a complete pipeline including re-ranking to measure end-to-end quality.
You can't optimize a production system without knowing how to measure it.
Agentic RAG builds on production RAG systems by adding autonomous planning and self-correction.
AI agents use LLMs as their reasoning core — you must understand LLMs before building agents.
Effective agent design requires advanced prompting techniques for reasoning and planning.
Tool use is how agents interact with the external world — it builds directly on agent fundamentals.
Workflows orchestrate agents and tools — understanding individual tool calls comes first.
Multi-agent systems coordinate multiple workflow instances — single-agent workflows must be mastered first.
Agentic RAG extends standard RAG with autonomous retrieval planning.
Experiment tracking records the metrics you learn to compute during model evaluation.
You can only serve a model you know how to build — supervised learning provides that foundation.
You monitor what you serve — model serving must be in place before monitoring makes sense.
CI/CD for ML automates the path from training to serving — serving knowledge is prerequisite.
Q-learning shares concepts with supervised learning (value estimation) — a strong ML foundation helps.
Policy gradient methods use neural networks as policy approximators.
RLHF uses policy gradient algorithms (PPO) to align LLMs — understanding PPO is essential.
RLHF is specifically about aligning LLMs — you need to know what an LLM is before aligning one.
